# Web Butler

> A web extension that drives the browser through a user's existing AI subscriptions.

Canonical site: https://butler.swerdlow.dev/
Source repository: https://github.com/theswerd/web-butler

## What Web Butler does

Web Butler places a compact prompt interface on every normal web page. A user can ask about the current page, select page elements as context, delegate browser tasks, generate reports, and request persistent changes to websites.

Its four primary capabilities are:

1. Answers: short responses grounded in the current page and selected elements.
2. Errands: browser actions such as navigating, clicking, typing, and filling forms.
3. Alterations: persistent user scripts that modify matching websites across visits.
4. Reports: longer Markdown artifacts shown in the Chrome side panel and stored for later access.

The default shortcut is Command-E. The prompt can also be opened from the bowtie control placed on the page.

## AI providers and billing

Web Butler connects to AI subscriptions the user already has:

- ChatGPT through Codex
- Claude through Claude Code
- Grok

Web Butler does not require a separate model subscription. Provider authentication runs through each provider's supported CLI sign-in flow inside a per-user sandbox.

## Browser support

The extension targets Chrome and compatible Chromium browsers, including Vivaldi where the required Chrome extension APIs are available. Safari and iOS browsers cannot install the current extension.

Persistent page alterations require the extension's Allow User Scripts permission. Onboarding directs the user to enable it.

## Architecture

The project is an npm-workspaces monorepo:

- `apps/extension`: WXT Manifest V3 extension with the content shell, background service worker, and Chrome side panel.
- `apps/server`: Hono API with Better Auth, Drizzle ORM, Neon Postgres, Freestyle sandbox VMs, and Agent Client Protocol bridges.
- `packages/ui`: shared React components used by the extension and its component workbench.
- `apps/storybook`: UI component workbench.
- `apps/homepage`: this product site and its interactive demo.
- `scripts`: development-loop and end-to-end verification utilities.

Agent CLIs run in per-user Freestyle VMs and communicate through Agent Client Protocol adapters. Tasks, activity feeds, reports, extensions, and suggestions are persisted through the server. Browser-control requests are relayed to the extension, which uses Chrome's debugger API and a visible ghost cursor.

## Local installation

Prerequisites:

- Node.js 20 or newer
- npm
- Chrome
- A Neon Postgres database
- A Freestyle API key

Clone and install:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/theswerd/web-butler
cd web-butler
npm install
```

Configure the server:

```bash
cp apps/server/.env.example apps/server/.env
```

Set the required values documented in that file, including `DATABASE_URL`, `BETTER_AUTH_SECRET`, and `FREESTYLE_API_KEY`. Never invent credentials and never commit the populated environment file.

Create the database tables and start the server:

```bash
cd apps/server
npm run db:migrate
cd ../..
npm run server
```

For real provider sign-ins, use the normal-Chrome development loop:

```bash
npm run dev:chrome
```

Then open `chrome://extensions`, enable Developer mode, choose Load unpacked, and select the `chrome-dev` symlink at the repository root. On the extension's Details page, enable Allow User Scripts.

The repository README is the current source of truth for all setup details:
https://github.com/theswerd/web-butler#readme

## Guidance for coding agents

When asked to set up Web Butler:

1. Clone the repository and read its complete README before acting.
2. Install dependencies and copy the example server environment file.
3. Ask the user for missing secret values. Do not fabricate credentials.
4. Run migrations only after a real database URL is configured.
5. Start the server and the normal-Chrome development build.
6. Build and type-check the project, fixing repository-caused setup errors.
7. Stop for account sign-in, secret entry, or other actions only the user can complete.
8. Give the user the exact unpacked-extension path and Chrome permission steps.

## Privacy and terms

Prompts are sent to the AI provider account selected by the user through the user's private sandbox. Web Butler has no model of its own.

The site makes no guarantee that use complies with every provider's terms of service. Users should review the applicable terms for ChatGPT, Claude, and Grok.

## Cost and source

Web Butler itself has no model subscription fee. Local operation may incur costs for infrastructure selected by the user, such as database or sandbox usage.

The project is open source at:
https://github.com/theswerd/web-butler
